chunks.text: In the latter half of the eighteenth century
an event began in Britain that would transform society and the economy from its foundation.
It was the dawn of the industrial revolution.
The steam engine, improved by James Watt, transformed the very concept of power.
Manufacturing, which had until then depended on rivers or on horses
could now run twenty - four hours a day on the power of coal and machines.
In Manchester and Liverpool, huge cotton textile factories were built one after another.
People who had until then worked the land in the countryside moved into the urban centers in search of work.
Urbanization advanced rapidly during this time.
But the labor condition in the factories was terrible.
Workdays of more than twelve hours, low wage
and children forced to work in the mines and the factories.
Child labor was the dark side of the society of that age.
After the middle of the nineteenth century
workers began to organize union and to strike for better treatment.
Through the efforts of social reformers, laws limiting child labor were passed one after another.
The industrial revolution greatly raised the living standard and the average lifespan of humanity.
At the same time, it brought deep gaps between rich and poor
and serious environmental pollution.
Our society today still lies on the path of the transformation that the revolution began.
